Your parent can continue in the program even if their income rises up to 20% above the eligibility guidelines after they're accepted.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: If your parent's income exceeds the guidelines by 20% or less, they may still qualify. The exact income limits change yearly, so call (808) 832-5169 to check current levels for their household size.

faqs.question: Does my parent need experience working with special needs children?
faqs.answer: No experience required. The program provides specialized training and ongoing supervision. Your parent needs willingness to learn and the physical/mental ability to work with children who have developmental delays, learning disabilities, or behavioral challenges.

faqs.question: Can my parent do this if they're already getting Social Security?
faqs.answer: Yes, Social Security doesn't disqualify them, but all income sources count toward the eligibility limit. The Foster Grandparent stipend is tax-free and shouldn't affect Social Security benefits.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives on a neighbor island?
faqs.answer: The program is statewide, but the main contact is the Oahu office at (808) 832-5169. They can connect your parent with services on other islands through the Department of Human Services.

faqs.question: How many hours must my parent work?
faqs.answer: Minimum 15 hours per week, typically up to 20 hours. This usually means about 4 hours per day. Your parent gets paid vacation and sick leave, unlike typical volunteer positions.

faqs.question: What disqualifies someone from this program?
faqs.answer: Any criminal history involving violence, sex offenses, drug/alcohol abuse, or child abuse/neglect. Also, being employed in the regular workforce or unable to pass the required physical exam.

programs.name: Memory Mentor Program
programs.tagline: Free volunteer visits and safety checks for Hawaii seniors with memory loss living alone.

localEntities.name: Catholic Charities Hawaiʻi

programs.intro:
If your parent has memory loss and lives alone in Hawaii with little support, they may qualify for free volunteer help through the Memory Mentor Program. Because memory issues make daily tasks harder and increase safety risks, this program provides regular friendly visits, safety checks, grocery shopping help, and reminders from trained volunteers.

The program targets seniors who are isolated and struggling with memory-related challenges but don't yet need comprehensive medical care. There are no income or asset requirements — just the need for basic in-home support and companionship.

summary: Little or no current caregiving support
otherRequirements: Must have memory loss
Must be living alone with minimal caregiving support

applicationGuide.summary: Call the Senior Intake Line — no formal application needed.

steps.description: Explain your parent's memory challenges, living situation, and what type of support would be most helpful.

steps.title: Wait for volunteer matching
steps.description: Catholic Charities will work to match your parent with an available volunteer mentor based on location and needs.

applicationGuide.tip: Be specific about your parent's memory challenges and safety concerns — this helps match them with the right volunteer.

offices.name: Catholic Charities Hawaiʻi - Senior Intake Line

contentSections.text: This is a volunteer-based program, so services depend on volunteer availability in your parent's area. If no volunteers are currently available, ask to be placed on a waiting list.

faqs.question: What specific help can the volunteers provide?
faqs.answer: Volunteers offer friendly visits, safety checks, grocery shopping assistance, help reviewing mail, and providing reminders for appointments or medications. This is basic companionship and support, not medical care.

faqs.question: How often will a volunteer visit my parent?
faqs.answer: Visit frequency isn't specified and likely depends on your parent's needs and volunteer availability. Discuss the ideal schedule during your intake call.

faqs.question: Does my parent need a formal memory loss diagnosis?
faqs.answer: The program description doesn't require a medical diagnosis — just observable memory challenges that make living alone difficult. The intake assessment will determine if your parent is a good fit.

faqs.question: Is this program available throughout Hawaii?
faqs.answer: The program is run by Catholic Charities Hawaiʻi, which primarily serves Oʻahu. Call to confirm if services are available in your parent's area on other islands.

faqs.question: What if my parent's condition gets worse and needs more care?
faqs.answer: Memory Mentor provides basic support, not comprehensive care. If your parent's needs increase, the volunteers may help identify other resources or programs, but you'll likely need additional services.

faqs.question: Is there a cost for this program?
faqs.answer: No, all Memory Mentor services are provided free of charge by volunteers through Catholic Charities Hawaiʻi.

stateOverview.intro:
Hawaii offers 21 programs to help your aging parent — from financial benefits that could save thousands yearly to free resources available regardless of income. The state's unique approach includes county-specific services (different help depending on whether your parent lives on Oahu, Big Island, Kaua'i, or Maui County) plus statewide programs like Med-QUEST Medicaid and SNAP food assistance. Most financial benefits have income limits around $2,000-3,000/month for a single person, but many support services are free to all seniors 60+.

Start with the programs below based on your parent's most urgent needs — whether that's paying for care, getting daily help at home, or navigating Medicare decisions. Each program has different application processes, so we'll show you exactly who to call and what to expect.

startHere.name: Med-QUEST (Medicaid for seniors/disabled)
startHere.why: Covers the biggest expenses — nursing home care, home modifications, and community support services for parents 65+ or disabled.

startHere.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
startHere.why: Monthly grocery assistance even for parents with income up to $2,998/month — one of the easier benefits to qualify for.

startHere.name: Kūpuna Care Program
startHere.why: Provides transportation, personal care, and meals for parents who need daily help but don't qualify for Medicaid.

startHere.name: Hawaii SHIP (State Health Insurance Assistance Program)
startHere.why: Free Medicare counseling can save hundreds monthly on prescription drugs and help navigate complex coverage decisions.

byNeed.need: Help paying for medical care and home services
byNeed.description: These programs cover the biggest expenses — nursing home costs, Medicare premiums, and daily care help at home.

byNeed.need: Daily support to help your parent stay home
byNeed.description: Get transportation, personal care, meal delivery, and companionship for parents who need help with daily activities.
Monthly assistance with groceries, energy bills, and free home energy upgrades like new water heaters and refrigerators.
Free counseling, legal advice, and advocacy when your parent faces problems with Medicare, housing, or care facilities.
Local programs that connect your parent to home care, meals, and support services specific to their island.

byNeed.need: Specialized support for memory loss or isolation
byNeed.description: Free volunteer visits and safety checks for seniors with memory loss or those who need companionship to stay home safely.

quickFacts: Hawaii has county-specific programs — different services on Oahu, Big Island, Kaua'i, and Maui County
Most financial benefits require income below $2,000-3,000/month for a single person
Many support services are free to all Hawaii residents 60+ regardless of income
The state's Executive Office on Aging coordinates most senior services statewide
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Hawaii divides help into two types: benefits that require income qualification (like Med-QUEST Medicaid for nursing home coverage or SNAP for groceries) and free resources available to all seniors 60+ (like Hawaii SHIP Medicare counseling, Senior Legal Helpline, or Memory Mentor visits). Start with free resources first — they require no paperwork and can connect you to qualified benefits your parent may not know about.
